original_abstract: Al-Būthī defines *al-maṣlaḥah* as any benefit intended by the Lawgiver (*al-Shāri*‘) for the welfare of His servants, specifically regarding the preservation of religion, life, intellect, lineage, and property, following a foundational hierarchy of necessity. Central to al-Būthī’s framework, as detailed in his seminal work *Ḍawābiṭ al-Maṣlaḥah fī al-Sharī‘ah al-Islāmiyyah*, is the assertion that *maṣlaḥah* can only function as a legitimate source of law if it adheres to five strict regulatory criteria (*ḍawābiṭ*). These criteria require that the interest: (a) falls within the scope of the five essential objectives (*maqāṣid*), (b) does not contradict the Qur’an, (c) does not contradict the Sunnah, (d) does not conflict with established *qiyās* (analogical reasoning), and (e) does not supersede a more significant or vital benefit. This paper examines these core principles while analyzing al-Būthī’s critique of al-Tūfī’s broader conceptualization of *maṣlaḥah*. Furthermore, the author provides a critical reassessment of al-Būthī’s internal classification and the specific limitations he imposes on the application of public interest in Islamic jurisprudence.
humanized_abstract: Al-Būthī states that the term *al-maṣlaḥah* refers to every benefit sought by the Lawgiver (al-Shāri‘) for the benefit of human beings, namely the safeguarding of the five fundamental human goals of the religion, which are faith, life, mind, progeny and wealth, all based on a hierarchical system of necessity. He emphasizes that *maṣlaḥah* may serve as a supplementary principle in Islamic Shariah only when it observes the five regulatory conditions that he calls *ḍawābiṭ*, that is, the interest should fall under one of the five basic human goals, that it should not run counter to the contradiction of the Shariah through the words of the Ouran, or the sayings of the Prophet, or through analogical deductions, nor that it should take precedence of another interest. This paper aims at elucidating these issues while discussing al-Būthī’s refutation of al-Tūfī’s broader theories concerning *maṣlaḥah*. It endeavours to examine and to discuss also al-Būthī’s classification of interests within the boundaries of Shariah and to evaluate the ruling principles that he set up to restrict the roles of public interest in Islamic law.

He emphasizes that *maṣlaḥah* may serve as a supplementary principle in Islamic Shariah only when it observes the five regulatory conditions that he calls *ḍawābiṭ*, that is, the interest should fall under one of the five basic human goals, that it should not run counter to the contradiction of the Shariah through the words of the Ouran, or the sayings of the Prophet, or through analogical deductions, nor that it should take precedence of another interest. This paper aims at elucidating these issues while discussing al-Būthī’s refutation of al-Tūfī’s broader theories concerning *maṣlaḥah*. It endeavours to examine and to discuss also al-Būthī’s classification of interests within the boundaries of Shariah and to evaluate the ruling principles that he set up to restrict the roles of public interest in Islamic law.
